title: Cost Segregation Study for Accelerated Depreciation

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must conduct engineering-based cost segregation study on commercial or rental real estate to reclassify building components into shorter recovery periods, accelerate depreciation deductions, and improve cash flow
criteria: Own commercial real estate, rental property, or building with construction, renovation, or acquisition cost over $500,000
Engage qualified cost segregation specialist or engineering firm to conduct detailed property analysis
Identify and reclassify components from 39-year (commercial) or 27.5-year (residential) to 5, 7, or 15-year property
Typical reclassifications: carpeting, wall coverings, specialized electrical, dedicated HVAC, signage, site improvements
Personal property (5-7 year) and land improvements (15-year) eligible for bonus depreciation and Section 179
Study can be performed on recently acquired property or properties owned for several years (catch-up depreciation)
File Form 3115 (Change in Accounting Method) if performing catch-up study on previously owned property
Typically beneficial when property cost exceeds $500,000 and owner has taxable income to offset

cost_segregation_benefits.accelerated_depreciation: Shift 20-40% of building basis from 27.5/39 year to 5/7/15 year recovery periods
cost_segregation_benefits.immediate_deductions: Reclassified 5-7 year property eligible for bonus depreciation and Section 179 expensing
cost_segregation_benefits.cash_flow_improvement: Front-loading depreciation reduces current taxes improving cash flow for investment
cost_segregation_benefits.catch_up_opportunity: Can perform study on property owned for years; claim catch-up depreciation via Form 3115
cost_segregation_benefits.bonus_depreciation_eligible: Short-life assets qualify for bonus depreciation providing immediate deduction
cost_segregation_benefits.roi_analysis: Study costs $5,000-$25,000+ but NPV of tax savings typically 5-20x study cost

restrictions: Study costs significant ($5,000-$25,000+); cost-benefit analysis important for smaller properties
IRS audit scrutiny if study not prepared by qualified engineer or lacks adequate support
Recapture risk if property sold before full depreciation; excess depreciation recaptured as ordinary income
State tax treatment may differ from federal; some states don't conform to bonus depreciation or Section 179
Residential rental property (27.5 years) has less potential than commercial (39 years) but still beneficial
Lower benefit in low-income years; optimal when business has taxable income to offset
Form 3115 filing required for catch-up depreciation on existing properties; automatic consent procedure applies
Must maintain adequate documentation and engineering support for reclassifications to defend in audit
